speaker: interviewee
text: So-- [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: --when I-- so I then came home from dinner, I said to her, [? Janka-- ?] her name was [? Janka-- ?] why should you go and leave a baby here? You have to nurse the baby, and you have your camp. I don't have anybody here, and I-- I'm single. And I could go and find out everything about your parents. So she agreed. She said, fine. And they did the same thing, right and there. They gave me all the documents, how to go, and who's going to wait for me, who's going to put me on this train, the other train. So I went all the way back-- I had to go back to [? Miechow ?] in order to go to her village. And I came to [? Miechow ?]. I ran into my neighbor. And he says, oh my god. What are you doing here? They look for you. They say that you are Russian partisan.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, I got scared. But I went into my house where I lived. And I had a-- when we left the farm, they gave me two little pigs to raise. So the pigs were in this-- in the barn, and they digged. Pigs like to dig. And they digged out a part with silver money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had this little sack. I put it in the little sack, and I had it in my house. So I figure-- I was just-- didn't thinking right, because it wasn't the right thing to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow, I took it with me in case an emergency, I need save my life or something. So I took it. And it was very dangerous to pass the guard with something carrying, because they could have come and checked what I carry. But I passed the guard, by the end of the city, and I went away. I walked, and walked. And the snow was very bad already that time. It wasn't the fall, close to winter already. And when I came to, to a-- the first house through the fields, because you couldn't see where the road, through the fields. I wore a pair of black boots, and they got white from the snow, all taken out the color. And I came into that house. But first, before I went into the house, I saw outside a stack with hay. And I pushed at my little sack with the money under that haystack. And I went in. And I went in, and I asked them [? what ?] I could sleep over. And they said, yes, you know, you could. But we don't have too much room. They had, behind the oven, they had a board like a bench. So you could sleep over there. You'll be warm, whatever. And they gave me to eat. They were such a poor people. They had nothing, even for dinner. They made a little flour with hot water mixed. And this is what we all ate. They ate it, and I ate it. They didn't even have any bread. So I sleeped over-- slept over the night. And that night, my father came in my-- in a dream I had. And my father brought me bread. He gave me a bread. Maybe because I didn't have any bread, whatever. That's why I dreamt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, why you give me just plain bread? How could I live. He says, yeah, anybody could survive on bread. So this is-- I had my father dream.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I slept over there, and I went further to go to Nowosiolki. I came to Nowosiolki. Her family, [? Janka's ?] family, was all OK. They didn't-- they did something in another village, but they didn't do in their village.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed with them, and those Russian partisans came back already from whatever they had to do in the back on the front. So they were back already. And then I had trouble already with them because they said that now, I am a spy. I was here, and now I'm coming back. I came spying after them. So they, you know, they didn't believe me what I said. So I told them who I have, and what I came for now, and where I was. So they came into that family. And they knew, you know, it was right. They showed them the letter that the son-- that they have [? in ?] [? despartisans. ?] So I stayed with them until the partisans said that they're leaving again. They, they were ordered to come to join the army instead, because the army, they're going to have an offensive on Kowel.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans were around Kowel. And they-- in Kowel, in the city. And the Russians were around Kowel. So they wanted-- the Germans wanted to break through, or the Russians wanted to break into the ghetto city. So they said they have to leave. But they-- I begged them to take me with. They said, no. They can't take me. They don't have any women. But I didn't care. They-- I saw them-- you know, all their trunk, and were maybe 20, 30 carts, buggies, horses and buggies, and horses they had. They were on horses. I went after them. I just walked, and walked, and walked after them. I said, they're not going to kill me. I just walked after them. When we came after two villages, at two or three villages, they asked me-- they stopped to eat. They asked me to come and eat with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, there was one captain that ran away from the, from, from the-- what is it called? When the Germans took it from-- the-- he was a soldier, and he was taken by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prisoner of war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prisoner of war. He run away. So he joined the partisans. And he was a captain. And he said to me that he has a friend over there in the village where when we-- we passed the Germans on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I should-- I could stay with her. And he gave me a letter to her. And when I came there, there were all Russians. And they were living in old houses because it was right on the front.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came in, and she took me in. And I stayed with them only for a few days. They, too, said that I am a spy. But I had that letter signed from the partisan captain and from other people there in the partisans. And so they took it that it was right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in a few days, we had to go again because they were making an offense of the Germans. So we had to evacuate the front. And we went away, further from the front. And that was Miernicza, a city, a town, and villages around there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she had friends over there, that woman. So we all walked over there, took whatever we could. And we just walked there. And we were already in the Russian side over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the war over for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- I was-- I was working for the Russians already when the war was over in May. Was it May the 5th? The war was over May. I was working already for the Russians. We made a big party, where I worked. I worked in a health department for, for the hospital, and ambulatories, and all kind of-- I was a bookkeeper there. So-- and then later, it started that I didn't want-- I could not-- because I worked with the Russians. And I saw how they felt about the Jews. It was not-- I didn't know the way it was over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I could not say I would-- I had a non-Jewish passport. I had a Ukrainian passport. And I was afraid already to tell them that I'm Jewish, because I, I was to [? work ?] already with them as a Ukrainian. They would too say that I'm a spy and send me some place, to Siberia. Yeah. It was very dangerous. So I didn't say that I am Jewish and I worked like this for them until we have-- had to leave. I had to leave to Poland. I wanted to leave to Poland. It was also a suggestion from my administrator-- who was an administrator. And he really was a person that lived over there. And he said to me, why would I-- if I would be you, why would I stay here? If I wouldn't have a family, would I stay here? Because conditions were very bad. So anyhow, I wanted to go to Poland to find out about my family. So-- and I needed a Polish passport. I could not go without a passport. So I went to-- just went into a priest and told them that I am Helena [? Vishnefska ?], I had the Polish passport. And I lived in this and this village. And I want to go to Poland to look for my family. So he gave me a birth certificate like. Not a passport, a birth certificate. So this is what I could already register to go to Poland. So you were [? Pesha ?], and then you were [? Katia, ?] and now you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Helena [? Vishnefska, ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Helena.

speaker: interviewee
text: To go to Poland. In Portland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Poland I was, for awhile, Helena [? Vishnefska ?]. Because--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you pick that name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just plain-- plain. It was an easy name. That's why I just picked it, while I was standing in the front of the priest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We just-- just like that, without any thought. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get back to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got in to Poland in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: That same summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In 19-- not summer. I came in the fall.

speaker: interviewer
text: The fall.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came in fall. Oh, yeah. 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you find?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, I went from city to city. Stayed two days here, two days there. I went to Lodz. I went to Lodz first, and I went in Ukraine also to cities before I went to Poland. I went to shuls to find somebody. I saw all the writing in the shuls where they could-- killed the Jews. They had blood writing on all the walls. They drew blood from the-- from themselves, and they were writing on the walls, names, stuff. And in Poland, I went from-- went first to Lodz, and I registered the Jewish [INAUDIBLE]. And I-- and then I went back to Lublin, which was close to our town, from the [INAUDIBLE]. I met a lot of people. I met-- in Lodz, I was walking at a street. I met a man from our city, which I, I couldn't believe it that he recognized me. I was a child. He was already a-- a married man with children. But he recognized me. And he just took me right away to his home. And then I walked next day, and I met a friend from Lodz already, there was in Lodz. And he has a ride with his sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed here just a few days, and a few days someplace else, maybe someplace else, somebody else. We just didn't have the patience to stay. You wanted to just find out whether somebody else lived from the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and then I went to wound up to be in [NON-ENGLISH] where I, I made a store. I made a grocery store. And I was there, working in the grocery store. I didn't have too much money. And I didn't have-- I had to go for my merchandise every day with my bicycle to buy the merchandise, because they, you know, they didn't know anybody, so they didn't trust you to give you [INAUDIBLE] every morning. I went, and I got for that money that I took in yesterday, I bought some merchandise for today. And that's the way it worked. And that's the way, I guess, I found out-- no, not me-- other people found out that I am there. And the people, a lot of people from our city came. My brother came from Russia with his wife. And he came over, somebody told him that they already saw me. And then later, I don't how, how-- some other people-- I mean, everybody from our city found out where I am. And they came. So it was already uncomfortable to be, for me, Polish. Because when my brother came, he didn't speak a good Polish. I-- they spoke Polish, but with accent. Jewish accent. But I didn't care. But after the Kielce progrom-- there was a Kielce pogrom that the Polish people made with the Jews in Kielce. And we all young people. Nobody knew that I was Jewish, but I wore the-- [MUFFLED SCRATCHING] Oh. I touched it. Sorry. They-- whoever I associated was Jewish. So they told me what's going on, and they are going to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I figured, this is what I should do. I should just leave, you know, with them, because my brother, he already. So we just could leave with my brother. And I started already to get ready to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I came in-- so they told me that from [? Chetzen ?], we could go to Berlin. And from there, we could go wherever we want. So when I came in to [? Chetzen ?] to make arrangements for me and my brother to leave, I found my husband staying behind the counter, [INAUDIBLE] He was the one that you were-- you should talk to. So that's what we did. You-- I stayed there maybe two days. We went back. And I gave my business to another couple that came-- not a couple, a sister and a brother. They came from Russia, and they needed a business. So I left this to them. And we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in Berlin. We got married in Berlin. My husband was still behind-- my husband was still in [? Chetzen ?], which I have a letter from him really-- that my family, my sisters with the families came in. I would just left a week or two that I left. They came in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --from Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a big wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: So they went-- well, we couldn't have a big wedding in Berlin. But-- you know, even our photographer didn't come in. But it was, it was beautiful. It was beautiful. I didn't have my family. See, I didn't want to get married. My husband wanted to get married before the war. And I said, I'm not getting married without my mother and father and my family. So I got married like this anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's going to be 50 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived all these adventures?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just luck. Just plain luck. I just-- I can't really tell you. There was no smartness. There was nothing. Because if somebody would just stop me and tell me, show me your passport, I would probably be red, and blue, and everything knowing that it's not a right one. Now, the first encounter that I had with that German, I mean, I could have been dead, but I would jump into that well, or he would kill me. Either way, because that was my decision.

speaker: interviewer
text: So see, every time, it was only luck. Now I only knew this woman, Prestupa, from school. And I used to-- then later, it happened to be lucky that they lived in the ghetto. So I went in there, and she used to give me food. I mean, buy. I the pay it for it. She brought in from the outside. And also, that they took me out. I mean, it was nothing of my-- Miracles.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it-- it was a miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Miracles.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a miracle, but it wasn't of my doing. Nothing of my doing. There were smarter people than I was. I was not-- I really wasn't-- I didn't do anything, really, to it. It just was the circumstances, and really, a miracle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why should I have the miracle and not somebody else? I mean it's-- so I, I really don't know. But everything that you see, there was none of my-- nothing of my doing. So-- and I guess it's with everybody else the same. It's with everybody else the same. And on that note, we'll stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll finish. And I'll thank you on behalf of--

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Steven Spielberg and the survivors of the Shoah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are my mother and father. And I got this from my sister, who sent me a little picture. So I, I had it done bigger.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I could--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Nuta Sekel and my mother's name is Leah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is my picture from before the war, when I was in Lodz. I-- Yeah, this is me and my husband and our daughter, Marilyn, who was born in Berlin. But they evacuated us from the Russian zone to the American zone, and we were in [? Hidenheim. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Gordon, with Marilyn, who was-- must have been about three months old here. She was born in Berlin. And we lived in the DP camp in [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I'm rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my wedding present, the only one that I got because we-- nobody had anything to give. They all bring, brought flowers. Except one man was in the antique business, so this is what he brought me for a gift. This is my husband, Gordon. Which-- we're going to have our 50th anniversary in September. And sitting with him is Shelly, my grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lewis, Mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Lewis. [LAUGHTER] Called him Shelly. That's Lewis, Shelly's son. Shelly's and Judy's son. And this is Faith Yablong, our granddaughter. And she's graduating high school this year. I'm very proud of them. And in the back-- I have to look who's there. It's Andrea, our granddaughter, in second year in high school. I'm very proud of her. And my daughter, Marilyn. And our son-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Sean. ?] [LAUGHTER] Ira and our son, Shelly. And our daughter-in-law, Judy. I'm very proud of them. I love them. And I like to be them-- I like to be with them all the time. And they're very helpful

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. They're very helpful.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: In all ways.

speaker: interviewee
text: To spend the money. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very proud of them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Girls, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted to say, I love being with my grandparents and spending time with them. And everything else. [MUSIC - HAPPY BIRTHDAY] ALL (SINGING): Happy birthday to you. Happy birthday to you. Happy birthday, dear Mommy. Happy birthday to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today is--

speaker: interviewee
text: The fifth of May.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much

speaker: interviewee
text: 39Th birthday. [LAUGHTER] And so we're very happy to be here to celebrate it, as we always celebrate all of our happy occasions together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: When do we get to eat the cake? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Shelly, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said before, I'm, I'm very proud of my parents. And I just owe them everything in the world, including being here, and the, the values they gave me, that they taught me. And I know that my mother is just a really extraordinary person. You really don't meet very many people like her. And I know that those are qualities that she learned about as a child. But also, she has incredible strength that she's developed through her life. And she's never betrayed, or taught us any sort of bitterness or anger, but really only to appreciate life, and appreciate all the gifts that are given to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if anyone's ever said this to you, but I really want to say that-- [LAUGHTER] --it's a great thing that you did in the way that you raised your daughter, because she's made my life easier in turn.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, thank you. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: You did a good job raising her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know what, that spills over, because I'm very proud of both of my kids, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, so am I.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it all stems from that. So I'm don't know if anyone's ever said it to you. I think I ought to be one of the first. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. We're proud of you, too, Ira.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, come on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Judy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marilyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, Judy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Judy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm, I'm just very happy to be part of the entire family, and to have the distinct privilege of being the mother of Bertha and Gordon's grandson, Lewis. And I'm looking forward to him carrying on the tradition of the Reis name, and the, you know, Jewish life in general.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Marilyn.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't talk. [LAUGHTER] Except I'll just say, I love both of my parents very much. And that's all. I can't. [LAUGHTER] It's the family disability. Emotional, very emotional.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] You cry in happy times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lewis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me? Oh. I'm happy-- be here. And I, I like my family a lot. They, they taught me a lot of good stuff. And I, I don't-- I-- they taught me a lot of the stuff I know now. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: How's Bubbe cooking? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Don't you say nothing about it? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You wanted to make a statement?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I just wanted to say that some people that not Jewish, and they hear from the Nazis, from everything else. And they don't care. They just think that everything happens to Jews what's going on, from the Nazis, and the skinheads, and stuff like that. Now, I was [? alive. ?] I was in the concentration camp. And I know what happened after the Jews were in there already. That they did the same thing. They tried to instigate the Ukrainians against the Polish people, and the Polish against the Ukrainians. And they were killing each other, even people didn't know each other. They used to go in at night and [INAUDIBLE] their whole family. And the same thing-- the Germans did all the-- a lot of bad things. When they used to go in a village if they didn't want to do something that they told them. They surrounded it, put it on fire, and let the people run wherever they wanted. They burned the whole village.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I just want everybody to know that it starts with Jews, but it does not finish with Jews. When the Jews weren't there, they would do everything else to other nationalities. So maybe, if peace starts with Jews, then that could also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe if peace in the world starts with Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe that could also spring.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Maybe that's the only solution to do to do to-- I hope that peace is starting now in Israel. And maybe it will be the solution for the whole world.

speaker: interviewee
text: May I say something?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: She is a real Jewish mother. She's a doctor, and a, a nurse, and a cook, and a, a sales [INAUDIBLE] girl. She is everything what you can only find. She does everything. And I'm just wondering from where it comes? I don't know. She is really-- when you want to buy, and you take something in your hand, she knows the value of it. And she knows the value of everything that I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Later on, I sit and I think to myself, from where it comes?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] But that's what she is. I know that-- bless somebody with the, with the whole thing. And I'm very proud.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather--

speaker: interviewee
text: I got her, and not somebody else got her. [LAUGHS] OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was a chemist already in those years. My grand-- my father's father was dying materials. So maybe that's where I got the touch of the hand, from materials.

speaker: interviewee
text: About gold, about diamonds, about everything she knows. I don't know from where it comes from, but she knows about it. She takes in hand. And she has it. It's a built in.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the years. The age that does that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on that happy note, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're all proud of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, I'm proud of you all, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could do that. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK?
